_protocol: Calibration topic — Houthi Red Sea posture by mid-2026. Populated 2026-04-11.

meta.title: Houthi Red Sea Posture by Mid-2026
meta.question: What will Houthi military posture toward Red Sea commercial shipping look like by July 2026?
meta.resolution: Assessed July 1, 2026 via: (1) 30-day rolling attack count against commercial vessels (attack frequency), (2) MARAD advisory status (Threat Level 1-4), (3) shipping insurance war-risk premium (% of vessel value), (4) weekly transit volume through Bab al-Mandeb as % of Jan 2024 baseline. H1 fires if ≥5 attacks/month AND >50% of Jan-2024 baseline transits re-routed. H2 fires if attacks selective (Israeli/US-linked targets only, ≤2 neutral-flag attacks/month). H3 fires if <1 commercial attack/month AND MARAD downgrades advisory. H4 fires if effective closure (≤20% of baseline transits, US NAVCENT NOTAM issued).

tagConfig._docs: ESCALATION tags push toward H1/H4. DEESCALATION tags push toward H3. SELECTIVE tags confirm H2. RHETORIC tags are attenuated by governor.

H1.label: Attacks resume at pre-ceasefire intensity (indiscriminate, ≥5/month)
H1.description: Houthis return to the late-2023/early-2024 campaign tempo: missile and drone strikes against any commercial vessel in or transiting the Red Sea, irrespective of flag or cargo. Threshold: ≥5 verified attacks against non-Israeli, non-US commercial shipping in a 30-day window, or public Houthi declaration ending political-screening policy. Precondition: Iran war escalates further OR Gaza conflict fully re-ignites OR Houthis perceive the ceasefire deal as a humiliation worth reversing for domestic mobilization.

H2.label: Selective targeting — political screening persists (current posture)
H2.description: Houthis maintain the post-May 2026 posture: attacks limited to vessels with Israeli, US, or UK links (ownership, flag, cargo destination). Neutral-flag, non-sanctioned shipping passes largely unmolested. Threshold: ≤2 neutral-flag commercial attacks/month AND ≥1 confirmed attack against Israeli/US-linked vessel. This is the current observed posture as of April 2026. Ceasefire with US (May 6) prevents American-shipping attacks but Houthis retain discretion on other targets. Most likely because it satisfies domestic mobilization narrative (solidarity with Palestine/Iran) while avoiding full US re-engagement.

H3.label: De facto ceasefire holds — no commercial attacks
H3.description: Houthis observe a broad commercial shipping ceasefire through July 2026, either through Iranian pressure following a larger regional settlement, US deterrence from Operation Rough Rider, or a formal extension of the May 6 deal to cover all commercial traffic. Threshold: <1 verified attack on any commercial vessel in the 30-day window before July 1, AND MARAD does not maintain a Threat Level ≥2 advisory for the Red Sea. Possible if the Iran-Israel/US war settles into frozen conflict and Houthis see no political upside to continued attacks.

H4.label: Escalation beyond Red Sea — Bab al-Mandeb effective closure
H4.description: Houthis escalate to a de facto closure of Bab al-Mandeb, using anti-ship missiles, mines, or coordinated drone swarms to push weekly transits below 20% of Jan 2024 baseline. Threshold: US NAVCENT issues NOTAM effectively closing the strait, OR Lloyd's/JWC declares Bab al-Mandeb a war-exclusion zone, OR ≥15 attacks/month for 2 consecutive months. Requires significant Iranian resupply (weapons), Houthi willingness to absorb sustained US/Saudi airstrikes, and a political logic (e.g., Iran directing maximum economic pressure). Very high cost given Operation Rough Rider precedent.

posteriorHistory.note: Initial priors. H2 anchored by current observed selective-targeting posture post-May 6 ceasefire. H3 non-trivial given US deterrence. H1 depressed because Houthis have incentive to maintain political-screening distinction. H4 low due to cost of Operation Rough Rider re-engagement.

Seven-month absence of Red Sea commercial attacks strongly supports H3 (de facto ceasefire holds). Houthi effort redirected to Israel strikes (not commercial shipping) and US-Houthi ceasefire terms specifically exempt American shipping. No Bab al-Mandeb closure evidence.

attack_tempo.label: Attack Frequency (30-day rolling)
attack_tempo.description: Verified anti-ship attacks in the Red Sea/Gulf of Aden, any vessel type. Includes missile, drone, boat swarm. Excludes Israeli airstrikes on Houthi positions.
attack_tempo.baseline_note: Peak tempo Jan-Feb 2024, ~12-15 attacks/month, mostly commercial vessels

transit_volume.label: Bab al-Mandeb Weekly Transits (% of Jan 2024 baseline)
transit_volume.description: Weekly commercial vessel transits through Bab al-Mandeb as a share of the Jan 2024 pre-campaign baseline. AIS-derived. Major carriers (MSC, Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd) still routing via Cape.
transit_volume.baseline_100pct_note: ~50-55 vessels/day in Jan 2024 before campaign began
transit_volume.source: MarineTraffic AIS, Kpler, Lloyd's List Intelligence

war_risk_premium.label: War Risk Insurance Premium (% of vessel value/voyage)
war_risk_premium.description: JWC/Lloyd's additional war risk premium for a single transit through the Red Sea (Suez to Bab al-Mandeb). Peaked ~1.5% in early 2024. A premium >1% signals commercial shipping community pricing in high attack probability.

marad_advisory.label: MARAD Red Sea Advisory Level
marad_advisory.description: US MARAD advisory threat level for the Red Sea/Bab al-Mandeb corridor. Level 3 = High threat, exercise extreme caution, avoid if possible. Level 4 = Critical, do not transit. Current level reflects continued elevated risk even under political-screening posture.

rough_rider_sortie_rate.label: Operation Rough Rider Strike Tempo (sorties/week)
rough_rider_sortie_rate.description: Estimated US airstrike sorties against Houthi military infrastructure in Yemen under Operation Rough Rider (commenced March 15, 2026). A sustained high tempo signals continued US deterrence pressure. Drop to near-zero would indicate ceasefire is holding OR US has shifted resources.

iran_resupply_estimate.label: Iranian Arms Resupply Confidence
iran_resupply_estimate.description: Confidence level that Iran is actively resupplying Houthis with anti-ship munitions (ballistic missiles, Shahed drones, naval mines). Iran is currently fighting a war with US/Israel and diverting production internally. LOW confidence in active resupply does not mean zero — stockpiles may be pre-positioned.
iran_resupply_estimate.source: USNI, DIA assessments, Israeli intelligence reporting

_design_rules: Each indicator must be OBSERVABLE — actions, not opinions
Each must have a clear fired/not-fired threshold
Every hypothesis needs at least one pro AND one anti indicator
Governor flags unfalsifiable hypotheses as DEGRADED

tier1_critical.desc: Three or more verified attacks on neutral-flag commercial vessels (non-Israeli, non-US, non-UK linked) within a single 30-day window. Threshold: UKMTO incident reports + independent AIS corroboration for ≥3 distinct vessels.
tier1_critical.note: Would confirm H1 — indiscriminate posture. Single incident may be targeting error; pattern of 3+ confirms policy shift.

tier1_critical.desc: US NAVCENT or IMO issues effective closure NOTAM/circular for Bab al-Mandeb, OR Lloyd's JWC declares the strait a war exclusion zone. Must be an official published advisory, not informal guidance.
tier1_critical.note: Definitive H4 indicator. Has not occurred even at peak 2024 attack tempo — would require major escalation.

tier1_critical.desc: Houthi leadership (Abdulmalik al-Houthi or senior Ansar Allah official) publicly announces cessation of all commercial shipping attacks, AND MARAD downgrades Red Sea advisory to Level 1 or 2 within 30 days.
tier1_critical.note: Would strongly confirm H3. Requires both Houthi announcement AND MARAD verification — announcement alone is RHETORIC.

tier1_critical.desc: Credible intelligence (2+ independent sources, not Houthi rhetoric) that Iran's IRGC-Quds Force has directed Houthis to stand down all anti-ship operations as part of a regional settlement.
tier1_critical.note: Iran controls Houthi weapons resupply and provides strategic direction. An Iranian directive is the highest-confidence H3 signal.

tier2_strong.desc: 30-day rolling attack count reaches ≥5 verified incidents (any vessel type) and holds above 5 for two consecutive months. Attacks must be confirmed by UKMTO, not just Houthi claims.
tier2_strong.note: Sustained tempo required — single spike could be targeting error or one-off. Two-month persistence confirms operational policy.

tier2_strong.desc: JWC/Lloyd's Red Sea war risk premium exceeds 1.2% of hull value per voyage for 3+ consecutive weeks.
tier2_strong.note: Insurance market is a leading indicator — underwriters price in attack probability before ships are hit. Premium >1.2% indicates market believes attacks are likely, not just possible.

tier2_strong.desc: Weekly AIS-tracked transits through Bab al-Mandeb fall below 25% of Jan 2024 baseline and remain below 25% for 3+ weeks.
tier2_strong.note: Volume collapse independent of attack count — if shipping companies preemptively re-route, it signals they expect H1 or H4 regardless of current attack rate.

tier2_strong.desc: UKMTO confirms 2+ attacks in a 30-day window that are verifiably against Israeli/US/UK-linked vessels with zero confirmed neutral-flag incidents in the same window, AND Houthi communications explicitly reference political criteria.
tier2_strong.note: Confirms H2 operational pattern is active and policy-driven, not random. Requires both action evidence and stated criteria.

tier2_strong.desc: CENTCOM publicly confirms suspension or halt of Operation Rough Rider airstrikes against Yemen, citing ceasefire compliance.
tier2_strong.note: Rough Rider continuation is the primary US lever constraining Houthi escalation. A halt removes that constraint and is H1/H2 permissive.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: H1 +8pp, H2 +5pp, H3 +5pp, H4 +3pp (ambiguous — could enable escalation OR reflect a deal)

tier2_strong.desc: Any of MSC, Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd, or CMA CGM publicly announces resumption of regular Red Sea routing (not a one-off test transit).
tier2_strong.note: Major carriers have their own intelligence and legal exposure. A return signals their risk models see a durable H3 or low-H2 environment.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Houthi official (Yahya Sarea or equivalent) issues specific threat to expand attacks to all commercial shipping, explicitly abandoning political-screening language. Must be a named official statement, not social media noise.
tier3_suggestive.note: RHETORIC — attenuated by governor. Houthis have a history of not matching rhetoric to action (and vice versa). Weight at ~25% of OBSERVED equivalent.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Iran-US/Israel war significantly intensifies (new front opened, Iranian nuclear facility struck, or Iranian ballistic missile strike on US asset), changing Houthi strategic calculus.
tier3_suggestive.note: Iran escalation increases Houthi motivation to escalate in solidarity but does not guarantee it — depends on Iranian direction and Houthi capacity.

tier3_suggestive.desc: OSINT or US intelligence assessment reports Houthi anti-ship drone/missile stockpiles have been significantly replenished after Rough Rider attrition.
tier3_suggestive.note: Capacity indicator — resupply is a necessary but not sufficient condition for H1/H4. Depressed stockpiles constrain escalation options.

tier3_suggestive.desc: MARAD downgrades Red Sea advisory from Level 3 to Level 2 (citing reduced threat frequency).
tier3_suggestive.note: MARAD advisories lag actual attack rate but provide a useful institutional consensus signal. Downgrade without major carrier return is insufficient for H3 confirmation.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Oman confirms active mediation between Houthis and a US/Saudi interlocutor aimed at a formal Red Sea shipping agreement (beyond the May 6 US-only deal).
tier3_suggestive.note: Oman is the only viable back-channel. Active Omani mediation is a leading indicator of H3 — but agreements can collapse. Treat as suggestive, not confirmatory.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Reporting (2+ sources) that Saudi Arabia has resumed or increased financial payments to Houthi-controlled entities in Yemen as part of a broader Yemen de-escalation deal.
tier3_suggestive.note: Saudi money flow has historically correlated with Houthi operational restraint. Would support H3 trajectory but is not dispositive.

anti_indicators.desc: For two consecutive months, all UKMTO-confirmed attacks are against vessels with verified Israeli/US/UK links, with zero neutral-flag confirmed incidents and no Houthi statements abandoning political criteria.
anti_indicators.note: Sustained screening discipline for 60+ days makes H1 (indiscriminate) implausible as a July 2026 state.

anti_indicators.desc: Zero verified anti-ship attacks of any kind for 60+ consecutive days, confirmed by UKMTO silence and AIS data showing no evasive routing by vessels that would be H2 targets.
anti_indicators.note: If even Israeli-linked vessels transit unmolested for 60 days, selective targeting is no longer the operative posture — H3 has replaced it.

anti_indicators.desc: Any verified anti-ship attack occurs after a 60+ day quiet period that had appeared to confirm H3.
anti_indicators.note: Ceasefires with Houthis have historically been fragile. An attack after a sustained quiet period resets the assessment toward H2.

anti_indicators.desc: Operation Rough Rider continues at ≥5 sorties/week through June 2026, and Houthi missile/drone infrastructure assessments indicate significant degradation (>40% reduction in estimated stockpile).
anti_indicators.note: H4 (full closure) requires offensive capacity. Sustained Rough Rider attrition makes full-closure attempt operationally implausible without major resupply.
anti_indicators.lr_migration_warning: design prior drift >30% — review before next firing

anti_indicators.desc: Two or more credible intelligence reports indicate Iranian ballistic missile/drone production is entirely consumed by the Iran war effort, with no surplus for Houthi resupply.
anti_indicators.note: H4 requires Iranian resupply. If Iran is fully capacity-constrained, Houthis cannot sustain a closure campaign against US/Saudi air power.
anti_indicators.lr_migration_warning: design prior drift >30% — review before next firing

actorModel.description: Five key actors whose interactions determine Houthi posture. Houthis are not fully autonomous — Iranian patron relationship and US deterrence are the two binding constraints. Saudi Arabia matters as a potential off-ramp provider. Shipping companies operationalize the outcome through routing decisions.

houthis.name: Ansar Allah (Houthis)
houthis.role: Primary actor. Controls attack decisions, targeting criteria, and ceasefire compliance.
houthis.decisionStyle: Ideologically driven but tactically opportunistic. Abd al-Malik al-Houthi sets strategic direction; operational commanders have significant discretion on targeting. Not fully rational-actor — domestic mobilization narrative (resistance axis) sometimes overrides strategic cost-benefit.
biases: Solidarity framing: attacks framed as defending Gaza/Palestine provide domestic legitimacy — hard to abandon without narrative substitute
Underestimation of US/Saudi resolve: repeatedly surprised by Operation Rough Rider escalation; tends to test limits
Survivorship bias: has survived multiple prior military campaigns; may over-discount future damage
Iran dependency blindspot: Houthi leadership publicly downplays Iranian direction even when operationally dependent
filters: Iranian weapons resupply is a hard operational constraint — cannot sustain high-tempo campaign without IRGC pipeline
Saudi border pressure: if KSA resumes ground pressure in Marib/Hodeidah, Houthis must divert resources
US Rough Rider deterrence: continued airstrikes degrade launch capacity and impose decision costs
overrides: Israeli escalation: any Israeli strike on Yemeni territory would likely trigger immediate Houthi retaliation regardless of ceasefire
Gaza re-ignition: full collapse of Gaza ceasefire with mass civilian casualties would create strong domestic pressure to re-enter conflict
Iranian direct order: if IRGC Quds Force directly orders escalation as part of broader war strategy, Houthis will comply

iran_irgc.name: Iran / IRGC-Quds Force
iran_irgc.role: Patron and strategic director. Controls weapons resupply pipeline and provides strategic guidance. Not micromanaging Houthi operations but sets parameters.
iran_irgc.decisionStyle: Institutionally rational with Supreme Leader veto. Currently in a degraded state — fighting a war with US/Israel, absorbing airstrikes, managing domestic pressure. Strategic bandwidth for Houthi management is compressed. IRGC-QF acts through relationships, not real-time command.
biases: Maximum pressure doctrine: when not resource-constrained, default is to keep all fronts active to fragment US/Israeli attention
Escalation ladder management: Iran calibrates proxy escalation to stay below US red lines — has been miscalibrating more in 2025-26
Deniability preference: prefers Houthi autonomy narrative to maintain plausible deniability with third parties
filters: Current war with US/Israel creates strong incentive to avoid triggering further US escalation in Yemen
Weapons stockpile competition: Iranian missile/drone production may be prioritized for Iran's own defense over Houthi resupply
Diplomatic off-ramp pressure: Oman and others may be offering Iran incentives to restrain Houthis as part of broader settlement
overrides: If Iran is losing the war significantly (regime-threatening), may order Houthis to escalate as distraction/attrition strategy
If Iran reaches a ceasefire that includes Yemen, will direct Houthi stand-down

us_centcom.name: US CENTCOM / DoD
us_centcom.role: Deterrence enforcer via Operation Rough Rider. Negotiated the May 6 ceasefire covering US shipping.
us_centcom.decisionStyle: Institutional-bureaucratic with political override (Trump White House). CENTCOM prefers clear rules of engagement and defined success criteria. White House prefers transactional deals (May 6 model). Tension: CENTCOM wants to degrade Houthi capacity; White House wants to declare victory and reduce exposure.
biases: Deal-seeking: Trump administration demonstrated willingness to accept partial deals (US-only ceasefire) that leave allies exposed
Metrics focus: defining success by US shipping safety rather than broader Red Sea stability
Saudi deference: unlikely to commit ground forces; prefers air campaign and Saudi proxy pressure
filters: Congressional pressure on costs/casualties constrains sustained Rough Rider operations
Carrier availability: if Truman CSG rotates out without replacement, sortie rate drops
Israeli coordination: US will not act against Houthi interests if it directly undermines Israeli operations
overrides: Any Houthi attack on a US naval vessel would trigger immediate and significant escalation
Any attack on a US-flagged commercial vessel post-May 6 ceasefire would immediately void the deal

saudi_arabia.name: Saudi Arabia / MBS
saudi_arabia.role: Regional power with direct Yemen stake. Can offer Houthis financial incentives and political legitimacy in exchange for Red Sea restraint. Also has military capacity (air power) to pressure Houthi.
saudi_arabia.decisionStyle: MBS is decisive and transactional. Has been pursuing a Yemen exit since 2022 and has strong incentive to stabilize. Will deal with Houthis if the price is right — has done so before.
biases: Cost-minimization: nine years of Yemen war has been enormously costly; strong preference for any deal over continued conflict
Normalization priority: Saudi-Israel normalization track competes with Yemen de-escalation for US bandwidth
Face-saving requirement: cannot be seen domestically as capitulating to Houthis — needs to frame any deal as Houthi compliance
filters: Cannot act independently of US on Yemen — dependent on US logistics, intelligence, and political cover
Oil price sensitivity: Red Sea disruption raises Brent, which benefits Saudi revenue but hurts regional stability narrative
overrides: If Houthis attack Saudi oil infrastructure (Aramco pipelines, Ras Tanura), Saudi will re-enter conflict regardless of diplomatic track

shipping_companies.name: Global Shipping Companies (MSC, Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd, CMA CGM, COSCO)
shipping_companies.role: Operationalize the outcome — their routing decisions determine transit volume and thus whether H3/H4 are observationally distinguishable from H2.
shipping_companies.decisionStyle: Risk-actuarial. Route decisions driven by insurance costs, crew safety requirements (ITF guidance), and competitive dynamics (first-mover advantage in returning to Red Sea vs. cost savings of Cape route).
biases: Herd behavior: carriers tend to return en masse once one major player re-routes back — and avoid the route en masse when attacks start
Underreaction to political screening: companies may not trust that Houthi targeting criteria are stable enough to rely on
Cape route lock-in: after 18+ months of rerouting, some carriers have adjusted contracts, port calls, and crewing to Cape route — switching costs are now real
filters: JWC/Lloyd's war risk premium: if premium exceeds roughly 0.5-0.8% per voyage, most carriers find Cape route economical
ITF crew safety guidance: seafarer unions will block transits if ITF issues Red Sea travel advisory
Charterer pressure: cargo owners may demand Cape routing in contracts regardless of carrier preference
overrides: If a major carrier loses a vessel to attack after returning to Red Sea, entire industry reverses immediately

methodology: ACTIONS OVER RHETORIC — only verified UKMTO/AIS-confirmed attacks move posteriors. Houthi statements are tagged RHETORIC and attenuated.
TAG EVERYTHING — separate EVENT (confirmed attack), DATA (insurance rate, transit count), RHETORIC (Houthi/Iranian statements), INTEL (intelligence-derived), ANALYSIS (OSINT inference).
DON'T FRONT-RUN — set tripwires on indicators, let news confirm before updating posteriors.
SOCIALIZATION DETECTION — Houthis often announce shifts before acting. Watch for new framing language ('all shipping' vs 'Zionist shipping') as leading indicator.
IRAN FILTER — any Houthi shift in posture should be interrogated for Iranian direction. Houthi-autonomous escalation is less stable than Iranian-directed escalation.
INSURANCE LEADS ATTACKS — war risk premium movements precede attack volume changes. Monitor JWC advisories as early warning.

evidenceLog.text: Houthis halt attacks on international shipping and Israel following Gaza peace plan implementation. Commercial attacks paused after ~100 incidents since November 2023.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Strong H3 signal: de facto ceasefire established. Baseline for current posture assessment.
evidenceLog.note: Founding context. Attack pause was conditional on Gaza peace — not unconditional cessation.

evidenceLog.text: Maersk Line resumes Red Sea transit with successive successful trans-Suez crossings. First major carrier to return since crisis began.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: H3 +signal: major carrier return matches indicator t2_major_carrier_return. Demonstrates commercial confidence in ceasefire durability.
evidenceLog.note: Maersk returning is a high-conviction market signal — carriers risk vessels worth hundreds of millions.

evidenceLog.text: US-Israel launch strikes on Iran. Iran war begins. Houthis do NOT immediately resume Red Sea attacks — surprising given prior behavior and regional ally mobilization.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Ambiguous signal. Non-attack is notable given prior Houthi pattern of joining Iranian-aligned conflicts. Could indicate degraded capability (Rough Rider), Iranian restraint order, or strategic patience.
evidenceLog.note: Three weeks of silence post-Iran-war-start is anomalous. The National base trust: 0.50 (unknown in source_db).

evidenceLog.text: Houthis enter Iran war: fire ballistic missiles and drones at Israel. First offensive action since October 2025 pause. Targets are Israeli, NOT commercial shipping.
evidenceLog.source: The National / multiple wire services, March 28 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: H2 +signal: Houthis re-activate but with selective targeting (Israel only, not commercial). Consistent with H2 (political screening). Does NOT match H1 (indiscriminate) or H3 (continued ceasefire).
evidenceLog.note: Critical distinction: attacking Israel ≠ attacking commercial shipping. Separate threat vectors.

evidenceLog.text: US-Iran ceasefire agreed April 8. Houthis operating 'Bab al-Mandeb tollbooth' — screening ships via AIS for US/Israel ties, allowing others to pass. Ships transmitting declarations of no US/Israel affiliation. Pattern mirrors Iran's 'Tehran tollbooth' in Strait of Hormuz.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE pending confirmation. H2-directional: selective screening matches t2_selective_confirmed_pattern definition. However, single specialist source (Huax, not in source_db — trust 0.50), claimState PROPOSED. Indicator requires sustained 60+ day pattern confirmation. Posteriors HOLD until wire service corroboration or second independent source.
evidenceLog.note: Huax is maritime analytics firm — specialist source but not in source_db. Weight 0.85 reflects domain expertise with limited calibration history.

evidenceLog.text: Lloyd's Market Association: 'Time will tell whether it is a pause or a peace... highly unlikely that trade into the Gulf will simply resume. The region remains at heightened risk with none of the underlying tension resolved.'
evidenceLog.source: Lloyd's Market Association (Neil Roberts), via The National, April 8 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE — this is ANALYSIS/RHETORIC from insurance industry. Informative for market sentiment but not evidence of Houthi posture change.
evidenceLog.note: Tagged ANALYSIS. Lloyd's is authoritative on risk pricing but this is assessment, not observed action.

evidenceLog.text: Ali Akbar Velayati (former Iranian FM): 'The unified command of the resistance front views Bab al-Mandeb as it does Hormuz. If the White House dares to repeat its foolish mistakes, the flow of global energy and trade can be disrupted with a single move.'
evidenceLog.source: Velayati social media post, reported by The National
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE — pure RHETORIC. Threat posturing from retired Iranian official. Does not constitute operational intelligence.
evidenceLog.note: Velayati is influential but no longer in formal command. Statement is deterrence signaling, not operational directive.

evidenceLog.text: Iran pulls navy from Red Sea per ship tracking reports. Reduces direct Iranian naval presence near Houthi operating area.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: Ambiguous. Could signal: (a) Iran reducing escalation risk (H3 lean), (b) Iran ceding Bab al-Mandeb to Houthis as proxy (H2 lean), (c) ceasefire compliance. Single-source — needs confirmation.
evidenceLog.note: Ship and Bunker not in source_db. Specialist maritime publication. claimState PROPOSED pending wire service confirmation.

evidenceLog.text: Iran army warned it will block trade through the Red Sea, Gulf of Oman, and Sea of Oman if the US naval blockade on Iranian ports continues. This is an explicit threat to expand the Hormuz closure to Red Sea shipping lanes — directly relevant to Houthi operational calculus. US blockade entered day 2 with trade into/out of Iran "completely halted." Iran-US ceasefire expires April 21.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. H1/H4-directional (Iran Red Sea action would reactivate indiscriminate attacks or push toward Bab al-Mandeb closure). t3_iran_war_intensification requires new front opened, nuclear facility struck, or ballistic missile strike on US asset — a Red Sea threat is rhetoric, not action. Observable threshold not met. If Iran executes the threat, this indicator fires.
evidenceLog.note: Cross-reference hormuz-closure ev_133. Iran Red Sea threat would change Houthi strategic calculus by providing Iranian cover/coordination for expanded attacks. Watch for Houthi response or IRGC-QF directive.
